## [00:00 - 03:00]

Let's talk about the second foundation. The second foundation is volume. Now if I had to say something about volume, this is what I'd say. Volume is the lifeblood of your voice. This is the one foundation that gives every single other foundation life. If you use this foundation correctly, you can really grab people's attention and hold their focus. Now I always look at volume with a scale of 1 to 10. So 1 being very quiet and then 10 being obnoxiously loud. Sorry if you're wearing earphones. The first thing is you're going to come across as being more confident. The second thing is you come across with more authority. The third thing is you come across as having more vitality. Their facial expressions came to life. Their body language came to life. I hope you start to notice now that your voice is connected to your body and your body is connected to your voice. The moment you push your voice a little more you can't help but come to life with the other parts of your body. That's another reason why volume is so critical. However, every single time in my in-person classes when I push my students who live at a natural 3 out of 10 volume, I push them to about a 5 even. They often say to me, I felt like I was being too much. I was being too much wasn't I? That was definitely too much wasn't it? And I said to them no. As well as the rest of the class, the rest of the class you hear them audibly say no. That was so engaging. So there's two things that happen here. The first thing is we often worry so much about what other people think about us that we cripple ourselves from progress. And that's the first thing that there's an unlock that happens for a lot of my students in my in-person class. Because again when they say, oh I was being too much wasn't I? That came across really aggressive. That came across really overdone wasn't it? And the rest of the audience immediately corrects them and says no. That was extremely engaging. Again that was so lively, full of vitality. But the first thing is we fear that judgment which needs correcting. But then the second thing that happens is internally you've gotten so used to your habitual volume. You're used to a certain volume. Habitually you've been doing it for maybe the last 10, 20, 30, 40, maybe 50 years, maybe more. So for you now to change that behavior and change your habitual volume it's going to feel very strange. So I want you just to acknowledge how different it's going to feel and make sure in your head that you're not thinking it's fake or phony, that you're not thinking it's overdone or

too much. Because remember the universal law of 88 keys. We want to learn how to use the whole instrument in every way. So don't judge it as being fake or phony, don't judge it as being inauthentic. This is you exploring your instrument. That's the foundation of volume.
